1. Describe what is stored in memory, according to the exemplar model of stereotyping

## Learning Goals

By the end of this page, students should be able to:

- Describe the continuum model of impression formation
- Define categorization and individuation

### Sometimes you hear people say that stereotypes exist for a reason. 

And that's true, but the people who say that often want to imply or outright state that the reason is accuracy or a kernel of truth. 

### But that's not correct. 

> The reason stereotypes exist is laziness, or what we often call efficiency. 

If you want to accurately form an impression of another person, you have to put in a little more work in order to get to know them as an *individual*. 

> This is a process known as individuation. 

### The purpose of this video is to describe the continuum model of impression formation, 

which helps us understand when we will form impressions of others
* based on stereotypes, 
* and when we will form impressions of others based on them as individuals. 

Similar to the three-stage model of attribution, this model has three steps. 

1. First, we meet a new person we need to form an impression of. 
2. Second, we categorize that person into a social group and use that group's stereotypes to understand this new person. This is an automatic process. It is easy and efficient. And those facts are the reason we use stereotypes so often. 
3. Finally, if we have the ability and the motivation, then we will engage in the effortful and resource-intensive process of individuating the new person. 

* So it is easy and quick to stereotype everyone we meet, 
* and it is hard and effortful to individuate them, to get to know them for who they really are. 

### If we want to individuate a person, we have to be motivated to do so. 

That means we have to find them interesting, or they have to be relevant to our goals in some way. 

Perhaps they are our boss. It's important to know who your boss really is, so you're likely to individuate him or her.

But that's not enough to start individuating a person. 

We also have to have the ability to pay attention to their personal attributes. 

### If we don't have enough time, or we are distracted, we won't individuate a person, even if we are motivated to do so. 

So most of the time, we simply categorize people into a social group, and use that group's stereotypes to form an impression of them. 

But how do we determine which category to categorize someone into? 

Usually we simply categorize them into whatever group they look most similar to. 

But consider Zombie Batman. Which group would you categorize him into?

payload.caption: What happened to Batman in this story?

payload.markdown:
* Is he a zombie?
* A villain? 
* Part of the Justice League? 
* Or just the Batman? 

There are no wrong answers here, but I think he looks most similar to other Batmans, so that's probably how I would categorize him, and I would use the Batman stereotypes to form an impression of him. 

So even though Blackest Night Batman is a zombie, I still think of him as working hard to protect the people of Gotham from villainy. And we use a similar process when we categorize others. 

We tend to categorize them into the groups they look most similar to. 

## Add these to your notes

1. Describe the three steps of the Continuum Model of Impression Formation
2. Make sure to note which steps are automatic and which are controlled

## Learning Goals

By the end of this page, students should be able to:

- Describe each of the studies in Devine (1989)

### Let me tell you a story about my friend Donald. 

I want you to form an impression of Donald as you listen to the story. 

* I ran into my old acquaintance Donald the other day, and I decided to go over and visit him since, by coincidence, we took our vacations at the same time. 
* Soon after I arrived, a salesman knocked at the door, but Donald refused to let him enter. 
* He also told me that he was refusing to pay his rent until the landlord repaints his apartment. 
* We talked for a while, had lunch, and then went out for a ride. 
* We used my car since Donald's had broken down that morning, and he told the garage mechanic that he would have to go somewhere else if he couldn't fix his car that same day. 
* We went to a park for about an hour, then stopped at a hardware store. I was sort of preoccupied, but Donald bought some small gadget, and then I heard him demand his money back from the sales clerk. 
* I couldn't find what I was looking for, so we left and walked a few blocks to another store. 
* The Red Cross had set up a stand and asked us to donate blood. Donald lied, saying he had diabetes and therefore could not give blood. 
* It's funny that I hadn't noticed it before, but when we got to the store, we found that it had gone out of business. 
* It was getting kind of late, so I took Donald to pick up his car, and we agreed to meet again as soon as possible. 

### Donald is what's called "ambiguously aggressive." 

That means you could interpret all of his actions as aggressive, or you could say there are situational factors which caused him to act aggressively. 

> It turns out that one factor which causes us to see him as aggressive or not, is the schemas we have salient in our minds. 

If we have a schema related to aggressive active in our minds, while we listen to that story, then we tend to think of Donald as more aggressive than if we don't. 

### Way back in 1989, Divine showed that everyone, both people high and low in prejudice, know the stereotype of black people as aggressive. 

Now before you start arguing in your head that that was 30 years ago, here is a word cloud of the stereotypes students stated society believes are associated with Black people quite recently.

As you can see, attributes like criminal and aggressive still loom large. 

Now you may be thinking something like, "knowing the stereotypes is different from endorsing them," and I agree, but knowing the stereotypes still has some troubling consequences. 

### In a second study, 

Devine showed that making this schema for Black people active causes people to rate our ambiguously aggressive friend Donald as more aggressive. 

And that was true for people both high and low in prejudice. 

So people that were low in prejudice but knew the stereotypes were still influenced by those stereotypes. 

### Importantly, Devine's third study provides a measure of hope. 

1. Her first study showed that everyone knows the stereotypes. 
2. Her second study showed that those stereotypes are automatically activated by everyone. 
3. But her third study shows that when controlled processes are used, people low in prejudice can inhibit their stereotyping. 

* Specifically, she had participants both high and low in prejudice write down stereotypically Black names to activate their schema for Black people. 
* Then they wrote down the traits they believed were associated with black people. 
* And she found that people high in prejudice listed more negative traits about Black people. 

So to sum up, Devine showed that 

1. everyone knows stereotypes. 
2. Stereotypes are automatically activated for everyone. 
3. But if you are low in prejudice, you can inhibit them in situations that allow for controlled processing.

1. Describe each study in Devine's paper

## Learning Goals

By the end of this page, students should be able to:

- Describe how stereotype rebound leads to an increase in stereotyping

### Devine showed that everyone knows stereotypes, and those stereotypes are automatically activated for everyone. 

Devine also showed that negative stereotypes can be inhibited by people low in prejudice. 

> So from that, you might conclude that you should spend your time consciously trying to suppress the stereotypes you know. 

But some research suggests that may not be the best strategy. 

### In one study that investigated how perspective-taking could be used to reduce stereotyping, 

* half of the participants were told, "Previous research has demonstrated that thoughts and impressions are consistently influenced by stereotypic preconceptions, and therefore you should actively try to avoid thinking about the photographed target in such a manner." 

> These participants are called 'suppressors' because they are actively suppressing their stereotypes. 

* The other half of participants were told, "Rely on the cultural stereotype and not on your personal beliefs of the social group represented in the photograph while writing the narrative essay." 

> These participants are called 'expressors' because they are actively expressing cultural stereotypes. 

* After writing an essay, participants read about our ambiguously aggressive friend Donald. 
* Perhaps counterintuitively, the suppressors thought Donald was more aggressive than the expressors. 

### So actively working to suppress stereotypes can actually cause them to be more salient. 

That's because in order to suppress a stereotype, you have to constantly check and see if it's active. 

> You are actually making the stereotype salient by trying to suppress it

## Add these to your notes

1. Describe how stereotype rebound increases stereotyping

## Learning Goals

By the end of this page, students should be able to:

- Describe the prejudice reduction task described on this page

### On the previous page, I described research that shows actively suppressing stereotypes actually increases stereotype activation. 

But what about training and just saying no to stereotypes? 

To see why this strategy might work, let's think about the schemas we have for social categories. 

1. First, we activate the category, and 
2. then all the attributes are also salient. 

> What if we systematically practice saying no to all the attributes we don't want? 

* Kawakami and colleagues instructed half of their participants to just say no to pairings of Black faces and Black stereotypes, 
* and also to pairings of white faces and white stereotypes. 
* At the same time, participants also said yes to pairings of Black faces and white stereotypes, 
* as well as white faces and Black stereotypes.

### So during this task, participants are not only negating existing stereotypes, but also learning new ones. 

* The other half of participants were instructed to maintain their stereotypic knowledge by saying yes to pairings of Black faces with Black stereotypes, and white faces with white stereotypes. 
* In addition, these participants also said no to pairings of Black faces with white stereotypes, and white faces with Black stereotypes.

In this version of the task, participants reinforced their stereotyping of blacks and whites. 

* Researchers had participants in both conditions respond to over 500 pairings. 
* At the end, stereotype activation was reduced among participants trained to say no to stereotypes, 

> and this effect even lasted 24 hours. 

Later research showed that this effect is actually driven more strongly by participants saying yes to the counter-stereotypes than by saying no to the stereotypes, and it can also be enhanced by saying no like you *really* mean it. 

So just being bored in an experimental lab and saying no, no, no isn't as effective as saying "that's wrong" over and over again. 

## Add these to your notes

1. Describe this stereotype reduction task
2. Which model of stereotyping were the researchers using?

## Learning Goals

By the end of this page, students should be able to:

- Describe the prejudice reduction task described on this page

### What would you think if you were one of the participants in those studies where you say "YES" and "NO" to stereotypic pairings? 

* Wouldn't you feel like the experimenters were trying to make you think a certain way? 
* To make you believe certain things? 
* Wouldn't you feel like, to a certain extent, the experimenters were trying to influence you? 

It turns out that when we feel like we've been influenced, we engage in what are called *correction processes*, to control for being influenced, to reset our beliefs to what we believe they would be if we weren't influenced. 

### The purpose of this page is to show you a study that investigated how these correction processes influence hiring decisions after one of these extensive training programs. 

Kawakami and colleagues were interested in whether training to associate traditionally male stereotypes like 

* dominant, 
* independent, 
* ambitious, 
* and competitive 

with women, 

and traditionally female stereotypes like 

* friendly, 
* submissive, 
* dependent, and 
* caring 

with men, 

would reduce gender disparities in hiring decisions. 

Specifically, if participants were shown two resumes of equally qualified candidates, 

* but one was male 
* and the other female, 

would the training reduce the bias participants have in favor of hiring men so that more women are hired? 

### In this training program, 

* participants were shown the face of a man or woman above a traditionally male stereotype and a traditionally female stereotype. 
* If a woman's face was shown to participants, they were required to select a traditionally male stereotype. 
* But if a man's face was shown to participants, they were required to select a traditionally female stereotype. 

1. Among participants that only completed the training and did nothing else, less than a quarter selected the female candidate for the job. 

And remember, the male and female candidates are equally qualified. We should expect the female candidate to be chosen about 50% of the time based on merit. 

> What the researchers hypothesized was happening is that participants were controlling for the influence of the training. 

So if they could disconnect the training from the candidate selection task, the participants wouldn't feel the need to correct for the influence of the training. 

2. So the next group of participants completed the training and then did a filler task before choosing a male or female candidate for the job. 

Among these participants, about 50% chose the female candidate for the job, which is exactly what we should expect based on the merits. 

3. The other way we can disrupt these correction processes is derived from noticing that correction processes are controlled processes. 

> That means they are not efficient. 

If we can distract participants by having them complete a second task while they choose a candidate, then they shouldn't be able to correct for the influence of training. 

The researchers tested this by having the final group of participants watch a light to see whether a light turned on in the corner of their computer screen while they completed the candidate selection task. 

Every time the light turned on, they had to press a button. 

The light would turn off and they'd have to watch for the next time the light turned on. 

Over 70% of participants in this condition chose the female job candidate. 

This is because participants were not able to engage their controlled correction processes, so the automatic effect of the training loomed large. 

### To sum up, 

even though everyone knows cultural stereotypes and those stereotypes were automatically activated, there are steps we can take to reduce the impact of those stereotypes on important decisions like who gets hired for a job.

## Add these to your notes

1. Describe the two approaches to disrupting correction processes
2. Define correction processes

## Learning Goals

By the end of this page, students should be able to:

- Define realistic and symbolic threats
- Describe the Robbers Cave experiment

### What does it take for there to be conflict between groups? 

Does it take 

* a lot of provocation 
* or a little? 

### What are the absolute minimum requirements for group conflict? 

* Do you need physical differences between groups? 
* Do you need a real history of conflict between the groups? 
* Is it enough just to have some groups, 
* or do you need to also have some sort of competition? 

These are some of the questions that the classic Robber's Cave experiment conducted in 1954 by Sharif provides some answers to. 

* The participants in the study were 22 boys. 
* They arrived at the camp believing that there were only 11 boys at the camp though. 
* You see, the boys have been divided into two groups, but they didn't know the other group existed. 


1. So during this initial stage, the boys formed friendships by completing camp activities like hiking and swimming without any knowledge that there was another group. 

> In fact, the boys forged group identities by giving themselves names. 

The Eagles and the Rattlers, and creating flags and t-shirts that represented their group. 

2. During the next stage of the experiment, competition between the groups was introduced. 

> The boys competed against one another in games to earn, to win rewards, and it was clear that only one group would get a reward. There was no second place prize. 

This represents what is called a *realistic threat*. 

> The concern that another group will harm our health, take our group's resources, or our group's territories. 

This led to conflict between the two groups. 

The conflict ranged from verbal insults to ransacking cabins. 

At one point, researchers had to physically separate the boys. 

3. Finally, conflict was reduced by having the two groups of boys cooperate with one another towards superordinate goals. 

For example, at one point the researchers contrived to have a truck break down and arrange things so that only with the combined help of all the boys from both groups could it be pushed down the road. 

*Intergroup cooperation* like this reduced hostilities between the two groups of boys. 

### To sum up, 

realistic threats are perceptions that another group threatens our 
1. health, 
2. resources, 
3. or territories. 

Realistic threats, like the competitions between boys at Robber's Cave, led to conflict between the groups. 

But those conflicts can be reduced through intergroup cooperation and the adoption of superordinate goals. 

### But I want to introduce the idea of another type of threat. 

> Symbolic threat. 

Symbolic threats are *perceptions* that another group threatens our core values or our way of life. 

So not all group conflict is rooted in competition or realistic threats. 

Instead, *quite often*, group conflict is rooted in symbolic threats. 

A good example of this is opposition to equal marriage. 

> Equal marriage does not pose a realistic threat to any group. 

Marriage is not a limited resource that groups can compete over. 

More marriages just means there are more marriages. 

Yet, some people are strongly opposed to equal marriage. 

This is because they see it as a symbolic threat, as a threat to their core values or way of life.

## Add these to your notes

1. The definitions of realistic and symbolic threat
2. Describe the three stages of the Robbers Cave Experiments

Symbolic Threat: Perceptions that another group threatens our core values or our way of life

## Learning Goals

By the end of this page, students should be able to:

- State the minimum requirements for discrimination
- Define Social Identity theory and its main principles

### I have a little class announcement.

I was able to run your submissions to the first few discussion boards through a personality analyzer.

> And our class was categorized as what's known as overestimators.

> I can also tell you that the other online social classes were categorized as underestimators.

In the future, we'll talk a bit more about what those categories mean.

### But for now, take a look at these two paintings.

payload.caption: Joe's preferred painting is of a farm; Jack's preferred painting is of a window

payload.markdown:
* Joe is an overestimator, just like the students in this class, and he likes painting A.
* Jack is an underestimator, like the students in the other classes, and he likes painting B.

Now I want you to imagine you've just been given $15 to distribute between Joe and Jack.

### How do you do it?

* Do you give more money to Joe or to Jack?

### Social identity theory 

argues that people still show prejudice and discrimination,

* even if the groups in question are completely arbitrary.
* Even if they personally never receive any benefit from discrimination, and
* even if they've never met any other members of the arbitrary group.

### In other words, 

contrary to what *realistic conflict theory* argues, 

> discrimination doesn't require competition over scarce resources.

### It's enough just to perceive yourself as a member of a group.

Similar to the s*elf-serving bias* and *ultimate attribution error* discussed in
previous modules, social identity theory argues that we can categorize people,
including ourselves, into groups, and we identify with our in-group.

We compare our in-group to out-groups, and what we want to see is that our in-group is better, and our in-group is unique or special.

We want to see our in-group as positive, of course, but that's not enough.

We also want to see our group as distinct.

So in social identity theory, prejudice and discrimination is not just about derogating out-groups.

They're also about building up our in-group.

## Learning Goals

By the end of this page, students should be able to:

- Describe how aversive racism plays a role in how doctors treat Black patients
- Describe the types of discrimination Black people face when interacting with their own doctors

### I hope you've all had a chance to watch Luke Cage on Netflix. 

Cage is a Black superhero who seems to have super strength and extremely thick skin. 

For someone like me who studies stereotyping, it's so interesting that two of the powers Marvel gave Luke Cage were 

* increased strength and 
* thicker skin. 

> Both are stereotypes about Black people. 

Most people will list strong or athletic as stereotypes of Black people when you ask them, 

### But did you know 

that if you ask people to compare how thick the skin of Black people and white people is, they tend to report that Black people have thicker skin? 

### Kind of a weird stereotype, 

but it starts having more sinister implications when you think about medical professionals believing this. 

Personally, I found it quite surprising when I read a study which reported that 

> half of white medical students and residents literally believe that Black people have thicker skin. 

### What are the consequences of a medical professional believing something so ridiculous? 

1. One implication is that white medical doctors believe Black people feel less pain and therefore are prescribed less pain medication and in general receive less accurate medical treatments. 

> In fact, at the implicit level, white people associate Black people with words related to superhumans or superheroes. 

Researchers found implicit associations between Black people and the words 

* ghost, 
* paranormal, 
* spirit, 
* wizard, 
* supernatural, 
* magic, 
* and mystical. 

### In addition, measured explicitly, 

white participants are more likely to believe Black people can do magical things like suppress hunger and thirst. 

Well, maybe you may be thinking to yourself, your run-of-the-mill family doctor believes these types of ridiculous stereotypes and discriminates against Black people in their diagnoses and prescriptions, 

### but this type of thing can't happen when it comes to something as serious as cancer, right? 

In a study of 18 oncologists, they took the race IAT and then their sessions with 112 new Black patients were recorded. Higher implicit bias was related to 

* shorter interactions, 
* less patient-centered and supportive communication, 
* and patients having trouble remembering the contents of the session. 

> And perhaps most importantly, patients had less confidence in the recommended treatments when the doctor was high in implicit prejudice. 

And that last point is extremely important. 

If patients don't trust their doctors, it can be part of a self-fulfilling prophecy wherein doctors expect Black patients to be less likely to adhere to their recommendations, and as a result, they spend less time and are less supportive with their Black patients, and then the Black patients pick up on this, and they are less trusting of the doctor and therefore actually are less likely to adhere to the doctor's recommendations. 

### So I think there are a couple of points to make. 

1. One, don't fall into the trap of thinking only uneducated people are prejudiced. Remember, doctors regularly discriminate against Black people even when it comes to life-and-death situations like cancer. 
2. Two, if you're Black, you really should consider finding a Black doctor *if you can*. Researchers found that all doctors except Black doctors show implicit preference for whites. Black doctors do not have a preference for whites or Blacks.

2. Describe how stereotypes about Black people can lead to discrimination by medical doctors

## Learning Goals

By the end of this page, students should be able to:

- Describe how perspective-taking can be used to reduce bias

### Remember, discrimination is defined in this class as any unequal treatment based on group membership. 

That can range from 
* very troubling acts like writing medical prescriptions based on race, 
* to physical violence like in the Robbers' Cave experiment, 
* or, as we've talked about on previous pages, non-verbal behaviors that contribute to self-fulfilling prophecies. 

For example, 

1. interviewers sitting farther away from black job candidates because they think the black job applicants are less qualified, 
2. which leads to less smooth interactions and 
3. therefore worse interview performance. 

### The type of discrimination we'll be talking about in this video is a non-verbal behavior called approach behaviors. 

> These include things like arm movements designed to pull people towards yourself and sitting closer to black people. 

Research by Todd and colleagues from 2011 used perspective taking to reduce this type of discrimination. 

* Participants watched a short documentary about a day in the life of a Black person named Glenn. 
* Participants in the perspective taking condition were told to spend five minutes writing about Glenn by visualizing clearly and vividly what he might be thinking, feeling, and experiencing as he goes through the various activities depicted in the documentary. This should be a good method of getting participants to put themselves in Glenn's shoes. 
* Participants in the control condition were told to remain objective and emotionally detached while they wrote about Glenn's day. This should prevent participants from putting themselves in Glenn's shoes. 

> The researchers found that perspective taking increases tendencies to approach Black people measured via arm movements. 

In other words, participants were quicker to pull photos of Black people toward themselves after perspective taking. 

### It is also important to note that perspective taking had no impact on tendencies to approach whites. 

* In another study, participants were asked to help a Confederate practice for an interview. 
* The participants were asked to set up two chairs for the interview. 
* The distance between the chairs represents how close participants intended to sit to their partner during the interview. 
* Researchers measured this distance with a tape measure. 
* Participants in the perspective taking condition set the chairs closer to one another when they believed the person they were helping with the interview was Black. 
* But perspective taking had no impact on how close participants intended to sit to a white interviewee. 

### So, though discrimination can seem overwhelming, there are some simple things we can do to reduce it, including just putting in the effort to take the perspective of others.

## Learning Goals

By the end of this page, students should be able to:

- Define social facilitation

### Welcome to Module 5. 

Module 4 focused on the social psychology of different groups. 

* How do we feel about, 
* what do we think about, 
* and how do we treat 

people that belong to different groups than us? 

> Those are known as intergroup processes. 

Module 5 is all about *intragroup* processes. 

1. Intergroup refers to processes that happen between groups. 
2. Intragroup refers to processes that happen within a group. 

So I want to start this module with a little thought experiment about what constitutes a group. 

* If we see four people standing near each other, laughing and talking, we understand that they are a group. 
* But what about 20,000 people packed into an arena to watch a professional hockey game? Is the crowd a group? 

### What about the other end of the spectrum? 

* Can two people be a group? 
* What if they aren't talking to one another? 
* What if they aren't interacting in any way? 

> Are they still a group? 

In a later presentation, I'm going to provide an exact definition of group for this course. 

For now though, instead of thinking about the exact definition of a group, I want to think about what are the minimal requirements that must be met for us to start seeing the psychological consequences of being in a group. 

### To that end, let me ask you a question. 

> How fast can you take off your shoes? 

* What about when you're alone 
* versus when you're with another person 
* versus you're with another person that's actually watching you? 

Do any of those distinctions matter? 

> Could the presence of another person really make you take your shoes off faster or slower? 

### The answer is yes. 

Research related to *social facilitation* has found that we are faster to take our shoes off when someone is in the room with us compared to when we are alone. 

In fact, we are fastest *when that someone is in the room and is watching us*. 

So we already start seeing some of the effects of being in a group when someone is in the same room as us, even if we aren't interacting. 

### Let me ask you another question now. 

> How quickly can you put a robe on that ties backwards? 

* How about when you're alone 
* versus someone is in the room with you 
* versus that person in the room is watching you? 

Do you think the same pattern should occur in this situation as when you were taking off your shoes? 

### It turns out that the answer is no. 

Researchers found that people were quickest to tie the backwards-tying robe when they were alone. 

They were slowest when there was another person in the room watching them. 

### Let's think about why we get a different pattern of results here. 

> It has to do with how complicated the task is, or more specifically, how good we are at the task. 

*Social facilitation* refers to the tendency for people to do better on simple tasks and worse on complex tasks when they are in the presence of others. 

So people make us better at taking off our shoes, a task we do all the time, but they make us worse when trying to put on a robe that ties backwards, something we've likely never done before. 

Part of the reason for this is 

1. physiological arousal, which arises because we are concerned with being evaluated by others, 
2. and because the mere presence of others causes us to be alert and vigilant. 

This *physiological arousal* then enhances whatever our dominant responses or tendencies are. 

Think about the consequences of this principle next time you try a new magic trick in front of your family or friends. 

If you've just learned the trick, then you'll likely be nervous and perform badly in front of others. But if you've done the trick many times, you'll actually be smoother in front of others. 

### Physiological arousal will actually enhance your skills. 

## Add these to your notes

2. Describe how whether the response is dominant or not is related to the effect of social facilitation
3. Define intergroup processes
4. Define intragroup processes

payload.caption: Intergroup Processes: Processes that occur between two or more group. How other groups influence your group and how your group influences other groups
Intragroup Processes: Processes that occur within a group. How others in your own group influence you and how you influence your group.
Social Facilitation: The tendency for people to do better on simple tasks and worse on complex tasks, when they are in the presence of others
